literal: before the eyes; present to direct awareness
invariant_definition: *Pratyakṣa* is direct cognition, paradigmatically perception, but the term is broader than bare sensation. It names the cognition that arises in immediate presentation rather than through a sign-relation, and its analysis depends on each school's account of contact, conceptuality, and self-awareness. Nyāya makes *indriyārtha-sannikarṣa* central; Advaita redescribes perception through *antaḥkaraṇa-vṛtti*, *vṛtti-vyāpti*, and *phala-vyāpti*; Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) sharply distinguishes sense-perception from *sākṣi-pratyakṣa*; Buddhist epistemologists restrict genuine perception to the non-conceptual grasp of the particular. The term therefore sits at the junction of ontology and cognitive theory: what counts as directly present depends on what kind of real there is to be present. In Vedānta *pratyakṣa* is indispensable for ordinary life and argument, but it does not by itself disclose *brahman*; even when realization is called immediate, the schools do not mean that liberated knowledge is just another sensory episode.

per_school.definition: Advaita explains perceptual disclosure through a pramāṇa-generated *antaḥkaraṇa-vṛtti* that removes obscuration with respect to the appearing object, while the cognition so produced operates within the structure of empirical manifestation [[SB 184-186](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/184-186-pratyaksa-vrtti)]. Such *pratyakṣa* remains pragmatically authoritative in *vyavahāra* even though, from the standpoint of *brahmajñāna*, *pratyakṣa* and the other pramāṇas concern *bādhita* objects; accordingly perception is a *vidhātṛ* rather than a *niṣeddhṛ*, and the śruti of non-duality is not thereby contradicted [[SB 184-186](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/184-186-pratyaksa-vrtti)][[BS 1.4](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/mbs_1.4)].
primary_loci: Siddhānta-Bindu, discussion of pramāṇa-janya antaḥkaraṇa-vṛtti and bādhita-pratyakṣa

per_school.definition: Viśiṣṭādvaita denies that perception ever presents a *nirviśeṣa* real, since even *nirvikalpaka-pratyakṣa* apprehends only a *saviśeṣa vastu* and later determinate cognition depends upon that prior specification [[RVas 30](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_30)]. The school therefore rejects the thesis that perception grasps mere being but not difference, holding instead that the world-manifold stands by the force of its own pramāṇa and is not sublated by non-dual śruti [[RVas 64](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/RVas_64)][[Svs 31](cite://yamuna/samvit-siddhi/31)][[Svs 32](cite://yamuna/samvit-siddhi/32)].

In Tattva-vāda, perceptual immediacy is not exhausted by external sense-cognition, since the *sākṣin* directly grasps a cognition together with its *prāmāṇya* without dependence on a further instrument [[TT Prāmāṇyavāda 6](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda, jñapti-svatastva-lakṣaṇa)]. Vyāsatīrtha therefore identifies the relevant *sākṣin*, in dialectical terms, with the *anuvyavasāya* admitted by the tārkikas, securing a distinct register of *sākṣi-pratyakṣa* alongside ordinary perceptual awareness [[TT Prāmāṇyavāda 7](cite://vyasatirtha/tarka-tandava/Prāmāṇyavāda, sākṣi-clarification)].

Sāṃkhya defines *pratyakṣa* as cognition that marks out the form of an actually connected object, thereby making perceptual apprehension the primary route for what is manifest [[SS 1.89](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/1.89-pratyaksa-laksana)][[SK 4 comm.](cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/4-commentary-pratyaksa)]. The Kārikā tradition accordingly restricts perception to the *vyakta*, while *pradhāna* and *puruṣa*, being *atīndriya*, are established by *anumāna* rather than by *pratyakṣa* [[SK 6 comm.](cite://isvara-krsna/samkhya-karika/6-commentary-vyakta-pratyaksa)].

Yoga counts *pratyakṣa* among the three *pramāṇas* and glosses it as that *citta-vṛtti* in which, through the sense-channel, the external object impresses itself on mind in a determinative way [[YS 1.7](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/1.7-pramanani)][[YBh 1.7](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7-pratyaksa)]. Beyond this ordinary perception, Vyāsa also speaks of a higher yogic immediacy, *tat paraṃ pratyakṣam*, in *nirvitarka-samāpatti*, whose viṣaya is unavailable to ordinary perception and not reducible to *śruta* or *anumāna* [[YBh 1.43-1.49](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.43-1.49-tat-param-pratyaksam)].

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika defines *pratyakṣa* as a *vyavasāyātmaka jñāna* produced by *indriyārtha-sannikarṣa*, and the bhāṣya insists that the sūtra specifies precisely the distinctive causal complex of perception [[NS 1.1.4](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa)][[NBh 1.1.4](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa)]. Perception is thus a realist disclosure of the object reached in contact, rather than a merely internal episode or a śābda construction [[NBh 1.1.4](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa)].

Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā defines *pratyakṣa* as the arising of cognition when a present object stands in contact with a person's senses, and it marks such apprehension as *animitta* because it is the direct grasp of what is presently existent [[MS 1.1.4](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.4-pratyaksa)][[ŚBh 1.1.4](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa)]. Śabara accordingly distinguishes genuine perception from error by insisting that only cognition rooted in the relevant contact counts as *pratyakṣa*, whereas what later suffers *bādha* does not [[ŚBh 1.1.4](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.4-pratyaksa)].

Mādhyamaka does not deny conventional perception, but it denies that *pratyakṣa* can certify *bhāvas* as self-grounded entities, since even the supposed *pramāṇa* and perceiver are themselves empty [[VV 5](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/5-pratyaksa-sunyata)]. The path may culminate in direct seeing, as when Candrakīrti speaks of *pudgalanairātmya* as *pratyakṣaṃ dṛṣṭvā*, yet that immediacy is still a realization of *niḥsvabhāvatā* rather than a foundation for *svabhāva* [[MAv 6](cite://candrakirti/madhyamakavatara/6-pratyaksa-pudgalanairatmya)][[VV 5](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/5-pratyaksa-sunyata)].
primary_loci: Madhyamakāvatāra, first bhūmi prose on direct seeing of pudgala-nairātmya

per_school.definition: Yogācāra subjects ordinary claims of perceptual externality to critique by asking how a cognition can count as *pratyakṣa* of an external object when, at the very time one says *idaṃ me pratyakṣam*, that object is not actually seen apart from cognition itself [[Vimś 16](cite://vasubandhu/vimshatika/16-pratyaksa-buddhi)]. In the epistemological refinement of the tradition, *pratyakṣa* is defined as *kalpanāpoḍha* and tied to *svalakṣaṇa* alone, so that conceptual construction belongs elsewhere than genuine perception [[PV 2.75](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.75-svalaksana-pratyaksa)][[PV 2.123](cite://dharmakirti/pramana-varttika/2.123-kalpanapodha)].

For Pratyabhijñā, even in perception the differentiated object appears only as abiding within the *pramātṛ*, so apparent exteriority is an ātman-based manifestation rather than an independently self-standing outside [[ĪPK 1.5.1](cite://utpaladeva/ishvara-pratyabhijna-karika/1.5.1-pratyaksepi)][[IPV 1.5.1](cite://abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti-vimarsini/1.5.1-pratyaksepi)]. Abhinavagupta correspondingly says that perceptual proof does not produce or newly reveal the pot already shining forth; it only removes *moha*, since *pramāṇas* terminate in de-obscuration rather than in manufacturing the object [[IPV 4.1.17](cite://abhinavagupta/isvara-pratyabhijna-vivrti-vimarsini/4.1.17-mohapasarana)].

Jainism classifies knowledge fivefold and then states that these are the *pramāṇas*, with the first two, *mati* and *śruta*, counted as *parokṣa* and the remainder as *pratyakṣa* [[TAS 1.9-1.12](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-1.12-pratyaksa)]. *Pratyakṣa* therefore has a more restricted scope here than in Nyāya, designating the non-mediated modes beginning with *avadhi* rather than ordinary sensory cognition as such [[TAS 1.9-1.12](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-1.12-pratyaksa)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss 'perception' is usable only if one does not smuggle in a modern picture of private sense-data. *Pratyakṣa* carries specific commitments about contact, conceptual content, and what counts as directly present. It is not automatically equivalent to empiricist sensation, nor is every immediate cognition in Indian philosophy sensory.
